---
title: "//# sourceMappingURL=data:...;base64,..."
description: What the inline base64 sourceMappingURL comment means
---

## What you're seeing

At the bottom of a bundled JavaScript file you may find a comment like:

```js
//# sourceMappingURL=data:application/json;base64,eyJ2ZXJzaW9uIjoz...
```

This is an **inline source map**. Instead of shipping a separate `app.js.map`
file, the bundler base64-encodes the entire source map and embeds it directly in
the JS file as a `data:` URL. When DevTools loads the file, it decodes that blob
and uses it to map the minified/transpiled output back to your original source —
so breakpoints, stack traces, and the Sources panel show readable code.

## Is it reliable / deterministic?

Yes. The source map is generated deterministically by the bundler
(esbuild, Vite, Next.js/Turbopack, webpack, etc.) from the same inputs, so the
mapping is exact and reproducible. It is decoded by the browser, not guessed.

## Why it appears "per module" and not everywhere

You only see this comment **when inline source maps are enabled**. It is one of
several source-map strategies a bundler can use:

- **Inline** (`data:...;base64,...`) — embedded in each emitted module/chunk.
  Convenient (one file) but inflates file size.
- **External** (`//# sourceMappingURL=app.js.map`) — points to a separate
  `.map` file fetched on demand. Smaller JS, common in production.
- **None** — source maps disabled entirely (typical for hardened production
  builds); no comment at all.

So it is not "everywhere" — its presence simply tells you the build was
configured to inline source maps for that output. Different files in the same app
can use different strategies depending on how each was built.

<Callout type="info">
  Inline source maps are great for local development and debugging. For
  production, prefer external `.map` files (or upload them to your error
  tracker) so you ship smaller bundles while keeping symbolicated stack traces.
</Callout>
